☀
☀ Travel Guide 10 Nights · 4 Destinations · Mid-Range

Complete 10-Night Itinerary

Philippines

From Manila’s colonial walls through El Nido’s otherworldly turquoise lagoons, Cebu’s whale sharks and waterfalls, and Bohol’s Chocolate Hills and tiny tarsiers.

10Nights
4Destinations
~€880Est. Total
~€88Per Day
Manila
Days 1–3 · NAIA
Flight to Puerto Princesa ~1h 20m
El Nido, Palawan
Days 4–6
Van→PPS flight to Cebu ~1h
Cebu City
Days 7–8
Ferry ~2h
Bohol Island
Days 9–10
Ferry + flight via Cebu
Manila
Day 11 · NAIA depart
01

Day-by-Day Itinerary

01 Arrival

Arrive Manila — Intramuros & First Night

  • Land at NAIA Terminal 3 — buy Globe or Smart SIM at arrival (~€1.65 for 5 GB)
  • Grab to hotel — check in near Intramuros or BGC for the best base
  • Fort Santiago and Intramuros walls at golden hour — lit beautifully at dusk
  • Manila Bay sunset walk along Roxas Boulevard — one of Asia’s great sunsets
  • BGC (Bonifacio Global City) for dinner — modern, walkable, safe at night
Eat: Sarsa or Manam in BGC — both excellent introductions to Filipino cuisine. The lechon kawali at Manam is essential. Budget €6.60–13.30 per person.
02 Manila

Intramuros Deep Dive & Binondo

  • Fort Santiago guided tour — Spanish colonial fort, Rizal’s prison cell, the moat
  • Manila Cathedral & San Agustin Church (UNESCO) — both inside the walled city
  • Casa Manila Museum — beautifully restored Spanish colonial house
  • Binondo (the world’s oldest Chinatown, est. 1594) — food crawl through the lanes
  • Jones Bridge at dusk — view of the Pasig River and the old city skyline
Eat: Binondo food crawl: Dong Bei Dumplings, Café Mezzanine, and Fresh Lumpia at Sincerity Café. Finish with hopia and tikoy pastries from any of the old bakeries on Ongpin Street.
03 Manila

National Museum, Rizal Park & Fly to Palawan

  • National Museum of Fine Arts — free entry, Juan Luna’s Spoliarium (a masterpiece)
  • National Museum of Natural History — stunning tree of life atrium, Philippine biodiversity
  • Rizal Park: Jose Rizal monument & the grand ceremonial grounds
  • Afternoon: fly Manila (MNL) → Puerto Princesa (PPS) — ~1h 20m, Cebu Pacific
  • Van transfer Puerto Princesa → El Nido (~5h overnight, or stay a night in PPS)
Eat: Lunch near Rizal Park at Aristocrat Restaurant — a Manila institution since 1936, famous for barbecue chicken and kare-kare. Board the van well fed.
04 Arrive Palawan

Arrive El Nido — Settle In & Tour A

  • Arrive El Nido by van (morning) — check in near El Nido town
  • Afternoon Tour A: Big Lagoon kayaking & Small Lagoon swim-through
  • Shimizu Island snorkelling — parrotfish, sea turtles, bright coral
  • Seven Commandos Beach: powdery white sand, coconut palms, crystal water
  • Sunset at Corong-Corong beach — 15-min walk from town, stunning karst silhouettes
Eat: Artcafe El Nido for Filipino comfort food. Fresh grilled tuna at any beachside stall for €4.15–5.80. Cold San Miguel at the night market after sunset.
05 El Nido — Palawan

Tour C — Hidden Beaches & Secret Coves

  • Tour C: Hidden Beach — secret entrance through a crack in the karst cliff
  • Matinloc Shrine: hike to the cliff edge for panoramic Bacuit Bay views
  • Tapiutan Island snorkelling — one of El Nido’s best reef systems
  • Secret Beach: swim through a narrow cave entrance into a hidden private cove
  • El Nido night market — fresh tuna sashimi, grilled squid, local fruit
Eat: Lunch included on the bangka tour. Night market: tuna sashimi plate €2.50, grilled squid €2, fresh buko juice €0.85. Trattoria Altrove for a proper dinner — book ahead.
06 El Nido — Palawan

Nacpan Beach & Van South to Puerto Princesa

  • Morning: tricycle to Nacpan Beach — 4 km of near-empty white sand arc
  • Swim, paddleboard (€5/hr), or simply find a hammock under the palms
  • Las Cabanas Beach zipline over the bay on the way back
  • Afternoon: van departs El Nido → Puerto Princesa (~5h) for morning flight
  • Overnight in Puerto Princesa near the airport for an early check-in
Eat: Beach shacks at Nacpan for grilled chicken and fresh coconuts. Dinner in Puerto Princesa at Kinabuchs Grill — their crocodile sisig is a local speciality worth trying.
07 Travel + Cebu

Puerto Princesa → Cebu City

  • Early flight PPS → CEB (~1h) — AirAsia or Cebu Pacific
  • Check in near Cebu IT Park or Fuente Osmeña — the city’s best base
  • Heritage of Cebu Monument — the striking sculptural tableau of Cebu’s history
  • Magellan’s Cross (1521) & Basilica Minore del Santo Niño — oldest church in Asia
  • Larsian BBQ strip at Fuente Osmeña for dinner — smoky, cheap, and essential
Eat: Larsian BBQ: pork skewers (isaw) €0.25–0.33 each + puso (hanging rice) €0.08. Order 10 skewers and four rice parcels and call it a feast for PHP 250.
08 Cebu City

Oslob Whale Sharks, Tumalog Falls & Ferry to Bohol

  • Depart 4 AM — arrive Oslob by 6:30 AM for whale shark snorkelling at first light
  • Tumalog Falls: 20 min from Oslob — wide curtain waterfall into an emerald pool
  • Moalboal sardine run snorkel on the return (Pescador Island, ~€8.30)
  • Back in Cebu by mid-afternoon — quick stop at Zubuchon for lechon
  • Evening Oceanjet ferry Cebu Pier 1 → Tagbilaran, Bohol (~2h)
Eat: Roadside turo-turo before Oslob at 4 AM. Lunch at Moalboal beach. Zubuchon Metro Ayala for Cebu lechon — Bourdain’s best pig. Board the ferry full.
09 Bohol Island

Chocolate Hills, Tarsiers & Loboc River

  • Chocolate Hills observation deck — 1,776 perfectly cone-shaped hills across the valley
  • Philippine Tarsier Sanctuary (PTSS) — ethical encounter with the world’s smallest primate
  • Loboc River floating restaurant lunch — live band, slow green river, coconut trees
  • Mahogany Forest hanging bridge walk & zipline
  • Check in at Alona Beach, Panglao Island — 45 min from Tagbilaran
Eat: Loboc River floating lunch included (€7.50). Evening at Alona Beach: whole grilled grouper at any beachfront restaurant — order with garlic rice and cold Red Horse beer.
10 Final Day

Balicasag Snorkel & Ferry Back to Manila

  • Early morning snorkel at Balicasag Island — wall dives, sea turtles, reef sharks
  • Virgin Island white sandbar — disappearing strip of white sand at low tide
  • Ferry Tagbilaran → Cebu (~2h) — then fly CEB → MNL (~1h)
  • Arrive Manila NAIA — overnight in Manila or connect to international flight
  • Final night in BGC or Makati for last Filipino dinner before flying home
Eat: Last dinner in Manila: Toyo Eatery in Makati for inventive modern Filipino cuisine — one of Asia’s 50 Best Restaurants. Or Jollibee at NAIA for a true farewell Chickenjoy.
02

  • Alona Beach & Panglao Island

03

Essential Dishes

Nationwide Filipino
Lechon
Whole spit-roasted pig with crackling skin stuffed with lemongrass, garlic, and bay leaves. The pinnacle of Filipino celebration food. Cebu lechon (herb-stuffed, no sauce needed) is considered the finest version in the country — Anthony Bourdain agreed publicly.
→ Zubuchon (Cebu) · Rico’s Lechon (Cebu & Manila) · CNX Lechon
Adobo
Pork or chicken braised in vinegar, soy sauce, garlic, bay leaves, and black pepper until the liquid reduces to a sticky glaze. Every Filipino household has its own version — ratios shift by region, family, and cook. The definitive Filipino dish.
→ Any Filipino home, turo-turo, or karinderia · nationwide
Sinigang
Sour tamarind broth soup with pork ribs, shrimp, or fish and vegetables. The sourness is the point — it comes from tamarind, unripe mango, or kamias. Deeply comforting, restorative, and uniquely Filipino. Always served with white rice to soak up the broth.
→ Manam (Manila) · any Filipino restaurant nationwide
Kare-Kare
Slow-braised oxtail or tripe in a thick peanut sauce with banana blossom and eggplant. Always eaten with bagoong (fermented shrimp paste) on the side — the contrast between the rich peanut broth and the salty, funky bagoong is the entire dish.
→ Aristocrat Restaurant (Manila) · Kamayan (Manila)
Cebu Specialties
Sutukil
Not a dish but a dining style unique to Cebu: Su (sugba/grilled), Tu (tula/soup), Kil (kinilaw/ceviche). You pick fresh fish at the market, then choose your cooking method. The kinilaw — raw tuna cured in vinegar, ginger, and chilli — is spectacular when the fish is truly fresh.
→ Pasil Fish Port area · Sutukil restaurants near Carbon Market
Puso (Hanging Rice)
Diamond-shaped parcels of coconut-leaf-woven rice — a Cebu street food icon sold at Larsian BBQ and every outdoor grill stall. They absorb the smoky barbecue juices and cost €0.08 each. Eat four of them with two sticks of liempo (grilled pork belly) and call it a meal.
→ Larsian BBQ Fuente Osmena · any Cebu street BBQ
Ngohiong (Cebu Lumpia)
Cebu’s deep-fried spring roll stuffed with jicama, pork, and five-spice powder — a Chinese-influenced snack unique to Cebu. The crispy roll is dipped in a sweet-sour sauce. Found at street stalls and market vendors throughout the city.
→ Street vendors near Carbon Market · Ngohiong sa SM Cebu
Danggit Breakfast
Dried rabbitfish (danggit) — Cebu’s most famous breakfast ingredient. Flat-dried, fried until crispy, and served with garlic fried rice (sinangag) and fried egg. The smell is intense; the taste is deeply savoury and addictive. Vacuum-packed danggit makes an excellent edible souvenir.
→ Any Cebu hotel breakfast · Carbon Market vendors
Island & Street Eats
Halo-Halo
Shaved ice loaded with sweet beans, jelly cubes, macapuno (coconut sport), nata de coco, leche flan, and topped with a scoop of ube (purple yam) ice cream. The definitive Filipino summer dessert — chaotic, sweet, cooling. Essential on any hot day.
→ Razon’s (Manila) · Chowking · any roadside halo-halo stall
Isaw (Grilled Skewers)
Grilled chicken or pork intestines threaded on bamboo skewers and cooked over charcoal until charred and smoky. Dipped in spiced vinegar or a sweet-sour sauce. The quintessential Filipino street food — a staple outside schools, markets, and anywhere at night.
→ Any street BBQ stall · BGC food trucks · Quezon City food parks
Jollibee
The Filipino fast food chain that beats McDonald’s in its own country. The Chickenjoy (fried chicken) has near-cult status — juicy, crispy, served with gravy and rice. Jolly Spaghetti (sweet banana ketchup sauce with hot dogs) is an acquired taste. A cultural institution that every visitor should try at least once.
→ Everywhere · Cebu airport terminal · NAIA Terminal 3
Tuba (Fresh Coconut Toddy)
The traditional fermented coconut sap drink of the Visayas — collected fresh from palm trees each morning, slightly sweet and mildly alcoholic. Served in bamboo cups at roadside stalls in Bohol and Cebu. A deeply local experience unavailable anywhere else in the world.
→ Roadside tuba stalls in Bohol · ask your tricycle driver
04

When to Visit

Best Overall
Dry Season
November — May
The definitive best time for this itinerary. Palawan, Cebu, and Bohol are dry and sunny. December and January are the most comfortable months nationwide. Sea conditions are calm and visibility for snorkelling and diving is at its peak throughout this window.
  • Best diving and snorkelling visibility
  • Christmas season (Dec) — festive atmosphere across the country
  • Sinulog Festival in Cebu (3rd Sunday of January) — spectacular
  • El Nido island tours run reliably
Good Value
Shoulder: Late Dry
April — May
Still dry and excellent for all islands. April is Holy Week (Semana Santa) — a deeply important Filipino celebration with unique processions and traditions in every town. Crowds spike on Easter weekend but the rest of April is relatively quiet. Whale shark season in Oslob runs year-round.
  • Holy Week processions (April) — remarkable cultural event
  • Fewer international tourists than peak season
  • Temperatures at their highest ~34–36°C
  • Good deals on accommodation
Manageable
SW Monsoon
June — September
The southwest monsoon brings heavy afternoon rains but often clears by mid-morning. El Nido island tours still run on good-weather days. Typhoon season begins — most storms track north of Palawan, but check forecasts closely. Significant hotel discounts during this window.
  • Heavy afternoon rains but manageable
  • Typhoon risk June–November (track: north Philippines)
  • 30–50% hotel discounts apply
  • Palawan relatively protected from typhoons
Avoid
Typhoon Peak
October — November
October and November carry the highest typhoon risk for Luzon and the Visayas (Cebu, Bohol). The Philippines typically experiences 20+ typhoons per year — several make direct landfall. Palawan is more sheltered, but ferries and flights cancel with little notice during storm events.
  • Highest typhoon risk for Cebu and Luzon
  • Ferries cancel frequently — disrupts island itinerary
  • Palawan more sheltered than the Visayas
  • Travel insurance is essential during this window

Island hopping is weather-dependent. El Nido’s bangka boats cancel tours when waves exceed 1.5m — this can happen even in the dry season after strong winds. Always build one buffer day into your El Nido stay. Ferry crossings (Cebu–Bohol) also cancel in heavy weather — check the PAGASA weather forecast app daily and have a flexible plan for Day 7.

05

Getting Around

Between Islands
✈️
Domestic Flights (Cebu Pacific / AirAsia PH)
Essential for this itinerary — the Philippines is an archipelago of 7,641 islands and flying is how you move between them efficiently. Cebu Pacific and AirAsia Philippines serve all major routes: Manila (MNL) → Puerto Princesa (PPS), Puerto Princesa → Cebu (CEB), and Cebu → Manila for the return. Book 3–6 weeks ahead for the best fares. Baggage fees add up — pack light or pay for an allowance upfront.
MNL→PPS: ~€25–58 · PPS→CEB: ~€20–47 · cebupacificair.com
⛴️
Cebu ↔ Bohol (Fast Ferry)
Oceanjet and 2Go operate fast ferry services between Cebu City’s Pier 1 and Tagbilaran City, Bohol — approximately 2 hours. Slow ferries also run (3.5h) and are cheaper. Book online at oceanjet.net or 2go.com.ph and arrive at the pier 45 minutes before departure. Ferry tickets sell out during Holy Week and peak December dates.
Oceanjet fast ferry: ~€5.80–9.20 per way · Book online at oceanjet.net
🚣
Bangka Boats — El Nido Island Hopping
Traditional outrigger boats (bangka) are how you reach all islands around El Nido. Tours A, B, C, and D each visit different island clusters — book through your hotel or at El Nido’s official tourism office the evening before. Tours include snorkel gear, a packed lunch, and a guide. All tours depart from El Nido Beach at 8:30–9 AM and return by 4–5 PM.
Tour A/C/D: €20–30/person · Book at El Nido Tourism Office or hotel
Local Transport — No IC Card Needed
🚘
Grab — Manila & Cebu Only
Grab operates in Metro Manila and Cebu City — use it for all city rides in both places. It is safer, metered, and cashless. Do not take unmetered taxis in Manila or Cebu — tourist prices and meter manipulation are common. In El Nido and Bohol, Grab does not operate: use tricycles (motorbike sidecars) for all short-distance travel.
Manila short trip: ~€2–3.30 · Cebu City: ~€1.65–3 · Download before arriving
🛵
Tricycles & Habal-Habal — Islands
The tricycle (motorbike with a sidecar) is the primary local transport in El Nido and Bohol. Always agree on a price before getting in — short trips should be €0.85–1.35 for a tricycle. A habal-habal (motorbike taxi, you ride pillion) is faster and cheaper for solo travellers. In Bohol, rent a motorbike (~€6.60/day) for flexible Chocolate Hills and interior exploration.
Tricycle short trip: €0.85–1.35 · Motorbike rental Bohol: ~€6.60/day
💵
Cash (PHP) — Essential Everywhere
The Philippines is predominantly cash-based outside Manila and Cebu hotels. El Nido has only a handful of ATMs — BDO and Metrobank are the most reliable — and they frequently run out of cash during peak season. Withdraw sufficient PHP in Puerto Princesa or Manila before heading to El Nido. €1 ≈ 60 PHP. Always carry €16–33 in small bills for tricycles and market stalls.
BDO / Metrobank ATMs: best rates · Withdraw in Puerto Princesa before El Nido
06

Essential Chains & Shopping

🍜 Food & Convenience
🍗
Jollibee
~1,500 Philippine locations · 24h · national institution
The Filipino fast food chain that outsells McDonald’s in its home country. Chickenjoy fried chicken (crispy, juicy, with gravy and rice) has genuine cult status. Jolly Spaghetti uses a sweet Filipino-style tomato sauce with hot dog slices — polarising but culturally essential. The Yumburger and palabok fiesta noodles round out a uniquely Filipino menu that makes more sense the longer you stay. Also open 24h in many locations.
The Chickenjoy meal (chicken + rice + drink): €2–140. Order it at Cebu airport before flying home — it’s one of the better airport meals in Southeast Asia.
🏪
7-Eleven Philippines
~3,500 locations · 24 hours · SIM & essentials hub
Philippine 7-Elevens are where you buy SIM cards, top up load (prepaid phone credit), pay bills, and pick up cheap snacks. The hot food counter carries siopao (steamed pork buns), chicken sandwiches, and cup noodles. The in-store ATMs are widely available and accept foreign cards — helpful in a country where standalone ATMs can be unreliable. Also sells E-Wallet cash-in for GCash users.
Buy Globe or Smart Tourist SIMs at any 7-Eleven in NAIA Terminal 3 arrivals area (~€1.65–299 for 5–15 GB). Top up with additional load as needed at any 7-Eleven counter.
☕
Bo’s Coffee & Coffee Bean
Filipino specialty coffee · major malls nationwide
Bo’s Coffee is the Philippines’ leading homegrown specialty coffee chain — founded in Cebu in 1996, it sources Philippine-grown beans from Benguet, Sagada, and Bukidnon. The Barako (Philippine robusta) iced coffee is a must-try — thick, intensely flavoured, distinctly Filipino. Found in SM Malls, Ayala Centers, and Robinsons across the country. Good WiFi and reliable air conditioning.
Order the Iced Barako or Philippine Blend iced coffee — you’re drinking one of the world’s rarest and most historically significant coffee varieties.
🛍️ Shopping & Souvenirs
🏬
SM Mall & Ayala Center — Cebu
SM City Cebu · Ayala Center Cebu · air-conditioned refuge
Cebu’s two major malls are where to find everything: pharmacies, supermarkets, money changers, ATMs, phone repair, laundry services, and hundreds of food options. Ayala Center Cebu skews slightly more upscale and has a better curated food hall. SM City has more local brand options and the best Jollibee in the city. Both malls are tourist lifelines for logistics between island hops.
Best souvenir buys in Cebu malls: dried mangoes (PHP 80–150 per pack), dried danggit (vacuum-packed for travel), Cebu chocolate (Ralfe Gourmet), and Tablea (native drinking chocolate tablets).
🥭
Cebu Dried Mangoes & Tablea
The definitive Filipino edible souvenir
Cebu is famous for two food souvenirs above all others. Dried mangoes — sweet, chewy, slightly tart — are sold everywhere from airport kiosks to Carbon Market by the kilo. The best brands are Morin and Rosquillos. Tablea is the traditional Filipino drinking chocolate made from pure cacao tablets — dissolve in hot water or milk for a thick, slightly bitter, deeply aromatic drink that predates Spanish colonisation.
Buy dried mangoes at Carbon Market or SM Supermarket for the best price — 30–40% cheaper than airport duty-free. Tablea at Ralfe Gourmet or Cacao Culture Farm in Cebu.
🗺️
Intramuros Craft Shops & Ukay-Ukay — Manila
Colonial quarter · Binondo · vintage markets
Intramuros and the nearby Escolta Street area have a growing cluster of Filipino designer boutiques, heritage craft shops, and artisan goods stores. Casa Manila Museum shop sells quality traditional Filipino souvenirs. For the adventurous: ukay-ukay (second-hand clothing markets) in Divisoria and Quiapo offer extraordinary finds at extraordinary prices — name-brand clothing and vintage Filipino barong tagalog shirts for €0.85–200.
Best Manila souvenir: a hand-embroidered barong tagalog shirt (the Philippine formal shirt) from Barong World or Tesoro’s. Authentic, lightweight, and deeply meaningful.
07

Budget Breakdown

🏨 Accommodation (10 nights avg. $45/night)~€415
🍜 Food & Drink (~$25/day)~€230
✈️ Domestic flights (MNL→PPS + PPS→CEB)~€92
🚣 Island hopping tours (El Nido x3, Balicasag)~€74
⛴️ Ferries (Cebu↔Bohol x2) + local transport~€42
🎫 Activities (whale sharks, Chocolate Hills, etc.)~€51
🛍️ Shopping & Souvenirs~€55

* The Philippines is excellent value — €1 ≈ 60 PHP. Meals from €1.65 ($1.75) at a turo-turo to PHP 600+ ($10+) at a sit-down restaurant. El Nido accommodation is pricier than Cebu or Bohol. Always withdraw enough PHP before arriving in El Nido — ATMs are limited and frequently empty.

Estimated Total (Per Person)
~€960
Including domestic flights · 10 nights · Mid-range
  • Without domestic flights~€865
  • Daily spend excl. flights~€87/day
  • Budget traveler daily~€37–55
  • Mid-range (this plan)~€69–110/day
  • Oslob whale shark tour~€25
  • PHP exchange rate (EUR)~60 PHP/EUR
  • SIM card (Globe/Smart, 7 days)~€1.65 ($1.75)
08

Insider Tips

💸
Withdraw Enough Cash Before El Nido

El Nido has only two or three ATMs and they frequently run out of cash during peak season (December–February). Withdraw sufficient PHP at Puerto Princesa airport or in Manila before travelling north. Carry small bills (€1.65, 200) for tricycles and market vendors. Most El Nido restaurants accept cash only — credit cards are rare outside upscale resorts.

⚠️
Build a Buffer Day into El Nido

Island hopping tours cancel when waves are rough — even during the dry season, a passing storm can shut tours for 24 hours. The van transfer from El Nido to Puerto Princesa (5h) can also be disrupted by road conditions. Always build at least one buffer day into your El Nido stay to absorb weather delays before your onward flight from Puerto Princesa.

🐋
Oslob Whale Sharks: Know the Trade-offs

Oslob is the easiest and most accessible whale shark encounter in Southeast Asia, but it’s controversial: the sharks are attracted by fish feeding, which changes their behaviour and natural migration. The more ethical alternative is Donsol in Sorsogon (Luzon) during November–June — wild sharks with no feeding. If doing Oslob, book through licensed operators only and strictly follow the no-touching guidelines.

📶
SIM Card & Connectivity Strategy

Buy a Globe or Smart Tourist SIM at NAIA Terminal 3 arrivals or any 7-Eleven (~€1.65–299 for 5–15 GB). Globe has marginally better coverage in Palawan. In El Nido, connectivity is limited and slow — download offline maps (Maps.me or Google Maps offline) before leaving Manila. Bohol and Cebu have good 4G coverage. Top up load at any 7-Eleven nationwide.

🌊
Reef-Safe Sunscreen Is Required

El Nido and Balicasag Island enforce reef-safe (no oxybenzone, no octinoxate) sunscreen policies — tour guides will check and confiscate non-compliant products. Buy biodegradable reef-safe sunscreen in Manila before departing or bring it from home. Wearing a rash guard reduces how much sunscreen you need — they sell cheaply at El Nido beachside shops.

🚬
Filipinos Are Exceptionally Welcoming

The Philippines consistently ranks among the world’s most hospitable countries — and the warmth is genuine, not performative. Locals in El Nido, Bohol, and the Visayas are genuinely curious and friendly. Learning a few words of Filipino (Tagalog) or Bisaya (Visayan) — even just "salamat" (thank you) — will be met with enormous appreciation and often a better price at the market.

Philippines Travel Guide · Manila · El Nido · Cebu · Bohol · 10 Nights · 7,641 Islands · Prices in EUR approx. · Always verify hours & book before travel
